I'm on page 96. I am at the close. I show you this page only because I like the third quote so well. A lot of people don't even know Zig had a brother who worked as a sales trainer. And we called Judge Zig's evil twin. They looked alike. They sounded alike, but kind of like the Duke twins. Here's a reference for you. Judge was kind of the opposite of Zig behaviorally. So Judge, five pack a day smoker, drink while he was working, hardcore direct sales guy. So Zig would never have said it, but Judge says you can't even wipe your ass with him. Maybe it takes money to buy toilet paper. I like the quote. It's pretty cool. Here we are finally at closing. And there's different kinds of closes. So there were a lot of questions. And again, Steve, there were a lot of people who asked your question. So who's this? All right, all the way from Kangaroo Land. So it would be great if you could talk about lead generation strategies from the stage. We're going to do road shows, selling consulting services in the room to lead gen form, to interview the closing meeting, sort of like the financial advisor model. I got a version of the question from a lot. So there's obviously different things we're going to close on with a presentation. One is all we're going for is an appointment, a next step commitment. So we'll talk about that first. So I am on your page 97, by the way. So for lead generation responses in a room or on a tele-seminar or on a webinar, often it pretty much only requires a very clear instruction. So specific to the financial advisors, for example, a lot of times the reason they wind up with these few appointments is because they weren't very clear. Now we're going to get your appointments booked. Here's the forms. Susan's back there. Take your form to Susan. Susan's going to set your appointment sometime in the next four days and give you the short list of things that would be ever so nice if you brought it with you to your appointment. Often they're not that direct and explicit. And so I said yesterday, one of the things to keep in mind about all of this is one of the things people are pretty good at, generally speaking, is following direct instructions. They're very good at standing in line. They all grade at that. I'm not, but most people are really good at it. I'm amazed when I go places and say, you know, they're going to stand at this monkey's concert. I mean, there's a million people in line to get in and they're being bossed around and they're in lines. And I'm thinking, you would think people would mute me at this. You know, I just went through somebody in a slot machine and waited until it was one minute before time to start. There was no line. Then you went in, you know, but they're in line for 30 minutes. Why? They got a reserved seat. Where's it going to go? You get it. But so most people are pretty good about following explicit instructions. So often the failure is just we didn't give them explicit instructions. Right? Second, alternate choice is usually better than single choice. So it's the appointment with choice of date and time. It might be the appointment or if you're not yet ready to schedule an appointment, we want to get you booked for a diagnostic call on the phone with one of our coaches. And so you want to go get under the blue flag with your form. All right? Alternate choice. You have to decide how much information you're going to request in this environment. Obviously, the more information you request, the bigger the impediment to scheduling a meeting, the less information, the less the impediment. So that's a faucet you have to decide what, you know, if your goal in life is the most number of appointments out of the room or off the webcast to diagnostic calls is humanly possible and you've got a bunch of salespeople who need to be fed and they can get blood out of a rock and you just, that's one thing. If you only got like one good salesperson or you're doing it and you only want to be on the phone or in front of a super qualified prospect, then you've got to be okay with driving 80% of them away and not having them registered in order to get the ones who do register to fill out a two-page form. Say something interesting. We actually are closing, not lead generating, but at the current version of the fill, grow, get rich in real estate weekend seminar. There's three or four different things sold, but then the last thing sold is the $100,000 two-year big dog thing. And they actually go in and meet one to one and somebody closes them. So early results, only three cities, but we've nearly doubled the closing percentage by forcing them to fill out a four-page questionnaire to go in the room and sit down with the closer. Interesting. You got to decide on reassurances versus reasons not to. So you will see, you know, the no salesman will call, copy has been around since there was a phone. So you got to decide, again, you don't want to make a promise you won't keep, but you know, if you come in and meet with us, we will never pursue you. We will never chase you. We won't go follow up with you. But our discussion either makes sense to you and you ask how to go forward. So you got to decide whether you want to bet that reassuring. And ultimately, you got to deal with procrastination versus urgency. So you got to think about how can we get them to do this right now, right? And there's either got to be scarcity, there's got to be exclusivity, there's got to be danger of loss from waiting, there's got to be a bribe that they want. The one thing I will tell you, even when all you are doing in a physical room is booking an appointment. It is better to have some thing that they walk out of there with than just an appointment slip. Just as you will sell more product if you have the product piled up, you will book more appointments if you have something piled up that they get to take home with them. Well, in your situation where you really are B2B and you are serious, I would make it more a relevant useful gift than I would a toy gift. Yeah, a tool of reference, but not something they have to get through before the appointment. Some other environments, yeah, it's the nice gift. It's the, you know, we got a limited number of these. I'm a patriot, Ronald Reagan coffee mugs and so the first 10 old people who come back and get their appointment taken care of with their financial advisor, you get this cup and you get a bib with a flag on it and you get a hurricane. I mean, there's nothing wrong with that either, right? So if you look at your page 98, this is a piece out of the video sales letter, not the one you saw. This is a piece out of the video sales letter for the free preview. So the close here is there. This is so we're still in the lead generation kind of game. They're registering to come to the free preview. There is a gift. It is a relevant gift. Then the other thing you need to know about driving them to appointments. One, they need to know what to expect at the appointment. So again, to use your example, the financial advisor, one of the reasons they muck it up and they don't get as many appointments as they think they should is because they sort of leave it vague what's going to happen when you come to the appointment and nobody likes uncertainty and they will generally imagine something more onerous and worse and difficult than it is. So they need to know exactly what's going to happen to them and what won't happen to them when they come to the appointment or the diagnostic call, which is usually the alternative if we're selling this at a distance that you want them to do. Secondly, they should be aware of some benefits that occur by the call or the appointment that occur whether or not they ever go any further than that or not because they're giving up time. So it would be cool if even if I don't like you and I don't want to do business with you after we meet or after I talked to you on the phone, I learned something, I know something I didn't know before that I can do something with independently. Alternative choices again, we talked about that. How much info to request, urgency procrastination, clear marching orders. So for purchase on 101, it's not that different. We want a clearly understood offer. So a lot of group presentations fall apart because people don't, they're just so confused about the offer itself. They don't know is it a seminar, is it a home study course, is it a pill, is it surgery, is it what the hell is it. So we want a good, clearly understood offer and any environment in which you can do it have a good order form. Albert's rule for print was you ought to spend as much time on the order form as you spend on the sales level. If you can control it at the back of the room, not only do you want a really good order form, but you want a really good crew. There's the biggest, worst thing about the Peter Law events is we had bad crews. So you're driving them back there and you've got terrible people with, well, anything from mediocre to terrible, it varied, but you didn't have good. Tommy Hopkins flew five in. So he put five of his people there on top of everybody else's people just to sort of shove them out of the way and do it. That basically was what was going on. So a good order form that restates the offer, the components, the value build. If you relied heavily on value build. Irresistible offers, of course, better the offer once you finally get to it to better the results you're going to get. So irresistible offers are all about the structure of price to value. Are we going to show price? Are we going to show individual price items that add up to some malarkey number that is then deeply discounted with some reason why? Jay Douglas Edwards called it the reduction to the ridiculous. You can sometimes do it by individual pricing. Sometimes you can do it by apples to oranges comparison. Here's a box with 10 seminars in it. And if you came to all 10 seminars, you would have spent $36,412 plus airfare in hotel rooms. And the whole thing is $499. We didn't compare apples to apples. We compared apples to price versus value. What's the value of the desire? So we can really talk about what will it be worth to you? Mastercard has this priceless campaign they run. It's the most successful ad campaign they've ever run. The value of this is priceless. Gift with purchase and toy in box. So gift with purchase almost always helps an offer because people like getting something for nothing. If they intellectually know there's something for nothing, it doesn't make any difference. Everybody is stimulated by the idea of getting something for nothing. So gift with purchase almost all we should be in an offer. The toy in a box, so if you're my age, you grew up not only with the toy in the Cracker Jock box, you grew up with toys in the cereal box. Cereals completely made out of sugar that children might eat had toys in them. Sometimes they weren't very good toys, but they were toys and the cereal company put them at the bottom of the box. So you either ate it or you dumped it out into like, I only did that once. You dumped it out into eight bowls and then put them back in the cupboard in order to get to the tie. This is the toy. This is a system I only used one time. But the toy in the cereal box is the thing they want more than anything else. So when you create the bonus, the gift with purchase, that is the one thing that they really want. So, like, if I'm selling the entire magnetic marketing system to Dentist, chiropractors pick a group, but we'll use Dentist. I am now these days in an uphill battle. You can actually feel the air deflate in the room. I had them right until I got to tools you have to use and things you have to learn. And then you could just feel the palpable disappointment in the room. There's ways to fix it that I'm unwilling to do, but at least I can do the and I have this cool appointment no sale letter sequence that the chiropractor used and they get 18% of the patients they don't get to accept case treatment to come back and I'm going to give you these three letters and you can use them exactly as you want. Well, that's what they really want in the first place. They just want something that they don't have to learn, do anything with, write their name, write their name on, hand out preferably, not even send out. We could just hand it out in the office. That would be even better. So the thing they want way back with Joe Polish in his whole kit sale, we determined that what they really wanted to do was see Joe do a carpet audit and a pitch. So we took that out of the product and we made it the gift with purchase for the top level, the B offer package. In magnetic marketing it was the at the low events, the pitches in the thing, but I think it was the package of special reports which are all very specific. One of which was how to get good mailing lists, which I had set up a big desire for lists and it's something nobody knows how to do. So they wanted that secret and it was the critique coupons because what they really wanted to send their shit to me, have me fix it, send it back to them. That's what they really want. So you got gift with purchase and then you got toy in the box. The toy in the box is the one thing they really want that will make them grudgingly buy and eat everything else in order to get it. You kind of want to figure out what that is. Speed of gratification. So who was I? Just having that conversation with Jack Dix yesterday and they have correctly figured out what to do. You don't mind, do you? So when they sell or now when we will sell, say to a financial advisor, the big whiz bang documentary film thing done on you and exclusive in your area, the big problem with that sale is you can't get a documentary film tomorrow. It's probably going to be a few months by the time you actually have a thing that you can use. So there's no speed in this thing. There's no immediate gratification. There's three or four things they do, much less elaborate, that actually can get done pretty quick. Like in a matter of days and then another one in a couple of weeks. So we can take two or three of those things and space them out. One that's like now, one that's in a few days and one that's in a few weeks to give people some gratification while they wait for the thing that they actually bought. The same reason it sells more if you've got stuff there. If you are selling through a webcast or a webinar and you're going to deliver a hard product, which I highly recommend, you may also want something that is immediately available digitally or that is the impending event that tomorrow night, the fast start class is and everybody's coming to fast start tomorrow, you want something that is fast. You will always help an offer. It comes with pain. You will always help an offer if you attach a tail to it. It's very reassuring. So even though the American Writers and Artists Certified Info Copywriter for Information Marketers course is as complete as it could possibly be. It's all there if you want as a freelance writer to target info marketers and get them as clients. The sale is infinitely helped if there's going to be two live Q&A calls with Dan. Giving you the calls on CD that happened before not as reassuring. It's very reassuring for the person to think I'm going to be able to get my questions answered. Now as a practical matter by the way, two thirds of people don't show up for the calls and a third of the people do. Most of them are too shy to ask a question and you really wind up answering questions for three people. But you still have to do the damn call so the tail is now recreating time and manual labor to something that does not require it in order for it to work. But it's very, very useful in the sale. When I did them, Magnetic Marketing and other products which I do not do now, the critique coupon drove a lot of sales. I know because anecdotally people would say it to you. This is the thing they waited to ask about. Now let me get this straight. I get this coupon and I can send you stuff and you will personally get it. That's exactly how it works. That tail drove a lot of the purchase activity. Fortunately, 60, 70% breakage, no redemption, but the 30% redemption that did occur, it's painful. I mean, painful. Not much backside benefit. Every once in a while it's about a whale. Somebody like it pick off as a client. So that made me feel a little better about it. But I mean, there were some months I'm doing 300 critiques. I mean, and you just want to, you know, and you can standardize some of it. But basically if Susan is sent in a nine page sales letter about selling persuasive voice training to plumbers, you know, and she is really butchered at, you know, I mean, it's really needs help. No check marks on a form are going to do. If you really want to sort of keep that promise, I got to write a page or two to Susan. I mean, it took time, you know, but it drives a lot of sales. So irresistible offer. Risk reversal, of course. Great offers have great risk reversal. The more of the sense of risk you take away, the more you can drive the sale. My overarching advice about that is don't make guarantees that you're going to be grumpy about honoring. Be willing to honor them even when the person is wrong, but then make the boldest, best guarantee that you possibly can. And understand guarantees, risk reversal is all math. None of it is emotion. Thus, you cannot be emotional about it. It's just math. That's all it is. Does it lift sales enough to more than justify its expense? If it does, end of story. Now, how wrong Susan is when she, you know, wants her refund and how late she is and how she only sent half the stuff back. And we can find her with Susan at eBay having made 56 copies. None of that matters. It's math. That's all it is. But the more you make risk go away, obviously, and if in your category you think there's no risk, sense of risk, you're wrong. Whatever it is you do, people are frightened of you. And they are frightened of themselves. So they either sense risk in doing business with you, or they sense risk in their ability to then do, use, benefit from the thing that you are selling there. I was in, there's a store in Amish country from here. If you're ever here with time on your hands, it's about an hour or 15 minute drive. It's worth visiting though. It's like a huge campus called Lehman. I mean, it's incredible. Oh, hi. Welcome to you. And I mean, so they sell it at the Amish, but they sell it to everybody. So they have some stuff in there you would never want, but it's kind of cool to see it. They're selling a cold powered refrigerator. I mean, it's like, really, this is cool. I mean, like there's everything in this store and you can spend two, three hours. There's a restaurant you can have lunch. I mean, it's like a day trip. So they have a catalog and they have a website. Is it Lehman.com? It's Lehman.com, isn't it? There you go. So you can find it. So we're down there, a couple of us. So one of the things that they had for sale, they got this thing. I have no idea if it works because, well, it's a punch line. So they got this thing that it's like it fakes out wasps somehow. So you hang it like on your deck and it kind of looks like a wasp nest. You just pop it and it hangs down and you hang it in the pitch on the box is wasps think this is a wasp nest and they don't want to get in a confrontation with other wasps because they're territorial and everybody sort of respects each other's territory. And so they see this thing and they leave you alone. So I buy two of them. And I hear, so I hear now right behind me, there's two people looking at it and one says to the other, the wife says to the husband and I'm sure she's right. Oh, you'll never hang those up. Leave them alone. And they walk away right now mine. I've had them for two years and I haven't hung them up. So. But see like I think I'm going to hang it up. But a lot of people say to themselves, oh, I'll never I'm never going to use that. I'm never going to do that. I wouldn't know how to hang it up. I wouldn't and they talk. So if you think that's not going on with what you sell, if it's going on with something as simple as a fake wasp nest that you take out of the box and go, it's going on with everything. So don't think you shouldn't have risk reversal. Ease of buying. So when you do presentation by however you do presentation, you want to be really careful about where now their attempt to buy is getting mucked up. So right before the last launch for Darcy, she's on the phone with our friends at infusion soft. Some part of this order taken thing. We're 15 minutes to air nine minutes to air whatever it is and it ain't working. I can only hear her into the conversation. But I hear no, I am not taking a ticket. So they have a system where you get a service ticket like an appliance repair deal and now you get in line and the techs pick up the tickets and they get back to you, I guess. And nine minutes to air. This is unacceptable, you know, but a lot of people are even check. I mean, the entire time I was on the road, I mean, I called to make sure the phone was working a lot. I didn't really want to talk to anybody. I just wanted to make sure it was working. I'd get to a hotel and first when I was checking in, I'd send a fax just to make sure it went through because I want to know my fax machines work. Because I don't trust anybody but me to make sure this thing is working. I'm still paranoid about these sorts of things. I mean, the Peter Low sales thing at the back was horrible. If you drive them to phones and you aren't controlling the phones, if you so, the ease of buying starts with who they have to deal with besides you, what they have to do to get an order. I was the only one, by the way, at the success events the entire nine years I was there who used my own order form. Gradually, Peter switched everybody to one big order form with everybody's stuff on it in two-point invisible and six NCR copies. And they filled it out and they swiped the cards and they piece out, not me. I got an order form and you just go back and you hand the order form in, you get your bag. We're not swiping your card, we're not doing any of that crap. Quick, quick, quick, simple. Here. And I always thought as long as you get me a phone number, you can screw up everything else. The address can be wrong, you could be dyslexic, you can spell the name wrong, I don't give a shit. We can fix it. Just get me a phone number, get their bag, get them out of the way so the next person can buy. Because there's 300 people standing in the line and they wanted to go home an hour and a half ago. The ones at the back of the line are walking away. I did a seminar many years ago for a buddy of Jim Newman, so I can't think of his name right now, Richard Souton. So Jim Newman wrote the book, Release Your Breaks, if you don't know what I'm talking about. Nelson, I told Nelson this. I did a church marketing seminar for pastors. You can laugh, it's all right. I'm not offended. So I was a featured speaker at a two-day event on church marketing. And I'm having lunch with a guy by the name of Jack Bowlin. Jack's passed away since. Nelson, did you know Jack? You know Jack? So I'm having lunch. There's like six or seven of us at a table and Jack Bowlin's at the table and Jack had a really big, non-denominational kind of new thought church outside of Detroit. Huge numbers. And I knew him because Foster Herbert got to work for us at Success Track, spoke there. Canfield has spoken there, Jim Newman. I don't know, is the church still? No. Went with Jack. So we're sitting at lunch and Jack says, here's a marketing problem for you. He says, we have somebody come in and speak and do a thing. And we got 3,000 people there and we got their stuff in the bookstore. Book, tapes, you know, whatever. And we create the stampede but the bottleneck is the bookstore. And people won't wait because they want to go have fried chicken and they want to go do whatever they want to do after church. So they all run there but they see the bottleneck and they say, well, I'll get it later or whatever and off they go. He says, so what do we do about that? So I'm being a wise ass, right? So I'm going to entertain myself. So with a completely straight face, I say, well, you know what they just started to do with the rental car places? Now you don't have to go in to check out. Some person comes out on roller skates with a little doohickey and skates up to you and wife says, I'm going to be in, wipes your card and sends you on your very way. So I'm being, you know, an ass, right? And I think no more about it until a month or so later, I get pictures in the mail of he's got people on roller skates with little doohickeys skating up and down the line, running your card and fetching your stuff. I said, this is hilarious. You know, I give them all the credit. I give them all the credit in the world. I get to Peter Lowell Vince. I had to bribe extra money, right? Got people out from behind the tables. So get out of there. Get out into the lines. Go to the back of the line and start getting their order forms and run it up and getting them their stuff. So we don't lose the back part of the line. Only one who ever did it. Everybody saw it even though I was last. Tommy saw it, Zig saw it. Peter saw it. Nobody. The first thing about this is let's beware of the sales prevention department. We create this rush of activity and then whoever is next in the way is figuring out how to stop people from buying. First time I heard it, I was speaking at an event and a person comes to the table. I didn't think this up myself. You don't see it on our order forms now. You did see it on mine during recession years. I'll do it now with a big ticket item. You'll still see it occasionally. But I learned it from a customer. I didn't know. It occurred to me. And it should have because I watched my mother do this for years. So customer comes to the table. I happened to overhear it. It wasn't Peter Lowell that it was years before that. Can I split this purchase on three credit cards? And of course the sales prevention department says no. I go, yes. Come with me. Now what exactly, well I know exactly how much I got on this one. And I know how much I got available on this one. And I don't have enough on any one of them. But if I use all three of them, great. I got it. Good. Here. We'll figure this out. And then I started putting it on order forms. So here you can tell us credit card charge this much to it. Credit card charge this much to it. Credit card charge this much to it. Did it all through the success track years? You'd be amazed how many dentists split a $399 purchase into put this much on this credit card, this much on this credit card. It should have occurred to me how much my mother did for years. Little notes on the back of all the credit card, keeping track of how much was left and available credit. You know, like 25 credit cards and there's $18 on this one and there's $42 on this one. And she wasn't alone, right? So, but you know, the corporate answer to that is 56 reasons why we can't do that. You know, we can only have one credit card for one purchase and we have to swipe it. So beware of the sales prevention activity that gets in your way. When you are selling to people over and over again, if you can just say, we have your credit card on file, just say yes. Right? Why not do that, right? Terms. So, there's a whole issue about whether you want to do installment payments, whether you want to do two-pay, whether you want to do three-pay. In some cases, you can hide it so you can get as many sales as you can in one pay and then you can go back to everybody that didn't buy who were at the presentation or who watched the webcast or who were on the tele-seminar and you can then give them the two-pay, the three-pay, the four-pay, the 52-pay option. In other cases where you can't, people fear the trade down. They fear if you do, if I say to you, you can do it in three-pay. Now everybody will do it in three-pay. This is a false fear. Now it will vary by the audience. I will grant you that. But my experience is, if I do three-pay and I don't do any incentive even to pre-pay, a huge number of people pre-pay. They still do it in one pay. A, they don't want to screw around with installment payments. The money item is not that big to them. The kind of person they are is that they don't buy on installments. And most of the time the trade in increased revenue is worth far more than the hassle and even what little trade down occurs. So if you think your number is a significant number to the people you are selling to, not a prohibitive barrier number, just a significant number. So a significant number for you, for me, for Trump. But there is some number at which we, for some thing, we go, if it is that number, you may want to do two-pay or three-pay. You also want to think about price structure if you do an AB offer where you do the gambit where the amount is the same, just the number of payments. So the basic offer is two payments of 200. The deluxe offer is four payments of 200. The person is still, they're just making a $200 choice. All you've done is extend the number of payments. Overall, the important thing is make buying as easy as possible when you are selling group, whether online or in a room, make it as easy as you possibly can for them to get the transaction done. And if it's big numbers, as fast as they possibly can, so see, if they even just wander off to the restroom, they may not come back. If they don't get taken care of pretty quick, now somebody is trying to talk them out of it. You know, it's why in most size groups, if I'm selling from the stage, I want order forms passed out. I don't want them going to the back to get an order form because everything moves faster if they have an opportunity to fill it out at the table while I'm finishing the pitch and while they're moving. Urgency, people will wait. So they're asked to believe scarcity. Key word here is believed. You have to find ways to make it believable. And if you sell to people repetitively via group presentations or really by any other media, you have to act in a way that does not have them stop believing you. So believed scarcity, believed exclusivity, believed price change if they wait, believed deadlines, a reason why for the above. So you can't say you can only have it if you do it right now and you could never have it if you don't do it right now if you don't have a reason for that. Otherwise, you won't be believed or you'll just be thought of as a mean, nasty, cruel SOB. The decisive action argument or other status argument. So if you can make acting now a self-esteem reinforcing thing and you can make not acting now the equivalent of going and standing under the white flag with a big loser on your forehead, you will get more people to act now. So you can do a wrap, one kind of wrap about another about the power of decisive action. Foster, because he talked about Napoleon Hill a lot and all the success track seminars he used the whole Andrew Carnegie, Napoleon Hill, unbeknownst to Napoleon Hill, Andrew Carnegie had a stopwatch under the desk, which I don't think they had stopwatches and that doesn't make any difference. He had a stopwatch under the desk and he gave Napoleon Hill only 60 seconds to say yes or no to his offer and after Napoleon Hill had not been a decisive individual, history itself would be changed and you would not have read a copy of thinking of Richard. We would never know the 17 laws of success and the same thing is true about you being able to. So he would do this whole wrap about Andrew Carnegie in the stopwatch. I have no idea if it's real or not. I don't know, I don't care, it doesn't make any difference to me. But you could build a wrap about that makes being decisive a self-esteem affirming act. So you want to put that in there and you can use of course impending events. By buying this you are coming to this, you are benefiting from this and it's happening tomorrow, next week, today, right? When we group sell area exclusive programs at the end of the discovery day, fast start days tomorrow. So you buy now, you're going to the victory dinner and then you're doing fast start tomorrow. You don't buy now, you're going home and you're not going to dinner, out. So that's loser winner and it's impending event. I only gave you one reference for clothes which is Zig's book, you really don't need any others. Having them is actually quite irrelevant. You have again two samples to look at. A current video sales letter, this one arthritis, pain goop, and the pieces of the 1999 script that match up. Again you will see structurally not much difference. So let's leap to page 120 where we will deal with some technical stuff in the last 15 minutes. In a physical environment, one, you pretty much want to block easy exit with product tables. Ideally you want them in the room, not outside the room. If they're outside the room, they're set up in a way that doesn't make it easy to get to the Johns or get out the door of the hotel. You don't want to let them stick it all the way down in their bookstore on the second floor, 500 miles away from the room that you're in. It makes a difference. It's the reason why you can't get off the Disney attraction without walking through the gift shop. Casino laid out, so you've got a snake to get to a restroom, past slot machines and gaming and bars. You can't just walk right to the restroom from wherever you are. There is never a direct path to a restroom in a casino from any place. You can't get there. They didn't do that because they were drunk when they designed a place. They did it because going or coming, they got a chance at you stopping and dumping some money in a machine. So where you put the sales activity matters. If you are in a big event, like the low events where I was, I was one of the great virtues of being last. We would have eight product sales areas. Up on the second floor of the arena, on the ground of the arena, there's a mess. Most of the time, I could get them to shut them all down and come to one on the floor during the time I was on stage because I was last. So I could pipe, pipe for everybody to one place. If there are multiple locations, use you to draw everybody as much as possible to one place. So, yep, you saw them all day long, folks. They're still there. There's six places you can go get this stuff. I'll be at that one. Come see me and we'll talk. Strong expectations conveyed. Again, tell people what the hell they're supposed to do. Don't be shy when you get to the close. Here's what's going to happen now. I will almost always, if I'm going to hang around somewhere in a physical environment, I'm happy to stay here and answer questions with you, talk to you as long as you would like, but go take care of business first. Get your stuff, then come see me. Mostly, they'll go do that. The ones you don't, they won't buy, period. So be rude to them and get them out of the way before the people that bought get there. You want a stampede. Physical stampede causes people to join the stampede. You've got to get them moving. You just have to. If they're not moving, it's difficult to close their sales. I saw Foster do it once. It's the most amazing thing I've ever seen. I would never have had to... Today, I wouldn't have the balls to do it. I mean, it's about 25 docks in a room. He turns them loose. They're supposed to come back and get their stuff. They got forms. He's done the... I'm filling the form out now with my pen and my American Express card. You take out... And I just thought these people have done nothing. And they don't get up at the break. They sit there. I'm in the back. I've never seen it. Never seen it before. Haven't seen it since. Nobody moves. He marches slowly, graciously, gentially to the back, gets a bundle of stuff, marches all the way back up to the front, down to the furthest guy at that table, and puts it in front of him. All right. Walks, goes all the way back. I'm serious. 30 times. Up, puts it down. Back, gets another one. Puts it down. Everybody's watching him. Nobody's moving. I'm standing in the back thinking, damn this thing I ever saw. I don't know where it came from. I don't know. I have no idea. It'll be really interesting to see what happens. It's probably not going to be good, but it'll be interesting. So he does this 30 times. Takes forever. And finally walks to the back and stands there. 100%. One after another. Filled the form out, came back, handed in the form. Nobody said it. Damn word. They went to the John, they came back, they sat down, they all had to stop. I ever saw anything like it. But usually, stampede. They join the stampede. So you got to think through how am I going to cause the stampede. Scarcity with the gift with purchase. We only have this number of this. Only the first 35 people are going to get the tail calls. You need the stampede. You will always sell more because people will join the stampede. They don't like to be the dummy sitting there while everybody else is running. Second bite at the apple. Structure your sales opportunity. Your speaking opportunity if you can so that you come back after the selling break. Now I never had the opportunity to do that at low events, but in most other environments and the ones I built, you could do that. We have it fulfilled now. The main sales break, when people go back and get their stuff, you want them coming back to the seats with the stuff. So now you are here and you got stuff. You are looking at it. You are taking it out of the bag. He has got stuff. A little annoying guy, he ain't got no stuff. He is like trying to look at their stuff. He kind of gets the message. Now in your next segment, you can just be dumb. You can speak and act as if everybody has got the stuff. Now take out your green thing and turn to page 37. Right now we are going to talk about what is on page 37. He can't do that because he ain't got no page 37. It is even better if it is something they get to take apart. So take out that thing and pull the red tab and the purple thing will fall off. And in there will be the, so they are all pulling out their shit. He ain't got no shit. To be amazed how many people at the end trudge back and get stuff because they have been embarrassed. They are disappointed and they are frustrated. And if you structure where there is multiple breaks, so there is another break and then you are coming back to do more stuff. There is a bunch of them who didn't get stuff, go get stuff because they have to come back and sit there again with no stuff. So they either leave or they get stuff. So it forces purchase of stuff, assumptive language, all during the pitch. Live webcasts. You are also striving for pressure. You don't have as easy an opportunity to create it. So you still need it. So the buy now button. The operators are standing by now. You see it in direct response TV sometimes where they show the call center. You will see it in the DRTV spot. You will see it in an infomercial. So they don't just say operators are standing by. They show you a bunch of operators. The reason they got the people on stage on camera are answering the phones. They could have them in another room somewhere and have more stage for the elephants and the dancing girls. But it is important to show you the people who are answering the phones. And the dumb ones have them sit there with no phone activity. Instead of coaching them, if there is no call coming in, pick up the phone and talk. Then put it down. It is okay. You get to be an actress. So operators standing by, strong expectation conveyed verbally and visually. On a webcast we still need scarcity of something. So again, one of the things you will see us do if you watch GKICs again and again and again, is you will see the new or additional gift with purchase added segment by segment by segment. Now we play fair. So everybody who bought at commercial break number one, when we come back towards the next commercial break, we add another something. And we give it to everybody who bought already. So it stacks. So nobody is penalized for being an early buyer. But every segment there is another thing added on. Because who is left? People who are resisting buying. So let's pile on some more. You can have, as I talked about earlier, you can have quasi-shills. Like if you are going to do a webcast and you are doing it to your own base, you want to take extra care to make sure some people are on the webcast to buy everything. So there will be after the first break, you will be able to say, and Susan from New York, thank you Susan and congratulations and Charlie. You know them all by name. The first ones, those are quasi-shills. They are not fakes, they are real buyers, but you made sure they were there. Recognition of buyers by name, countdown of the clock. Those are some of the things you can do with the live webcast. Applying to both, here are the presenters' tells that the audience reacts to more subconsciously than consciously. So the first tell that communicates to the audience or something wrong here, you probably really shouldn't buy this, etc., is the presenter who has a physiological change as he moves from what he thinks is content to what he thinks is commercial. When I got him, Foster was the worst I've ever seen in my life. Absolutely the worst. I see it a lot, but he was the worst. Meeting planner guy was running it actually. I was working for Tracy's uncle and Foster was on before me, a group of chiropractors, and I said to Rodney at the back of the room, I said he's the best speaker I've ever seen in my life, which still today that's true. And I said he's the worst salesperson, platform salesperson I have ever seen. A fire hydrant could do a better job than this. How the hell is it because I know Rodney ain't paying him. I said he's getting what he sells. I said how could he possibly make a living? And he wasn't. I mean so he did content, Steve, content, content, content, content, content, content. And he's a tall, kind of elegant, gentleman looking guy, nice suit, perfect. Good voice, kind of an old classic orator voice. You'll hear him if you were here for wealth on the CDs I gave you as a gift. So now it's time call to action. Here's Foster's call to action. The script's horrible, but as he does it, he like shrinks. And I mean he's sort of in a begging position. His voice drops a couple octaves, no eye contact. He's looking out into space. I mean it's horrible, horrible. If the pitch had been any longer than the one I'm going to be able to give you, he would be like in the Wizard of Oz. Eventually there's a pool laying there and he's gone. I mean the Wicked Witch gets dissolved and there's nothing there but a green glob of stuff. Here's his pitch. Some people ask me if they can have some of my material to take home with them. I mean I have cassette programs in that suitcase at the back of the room. Just take what you want and put in what you think it's worth. This is his pitch. I'm like oh my fucking lord. I fixed this guy by the way. I want you, I fixed this guy to a 95 to 99% close rate. Evening after evening after evening. It wasn't easy. But I see people do it all the time. They like not that radical but they physiologically shift as they are about to ask for money. And even if the audience doesn't consciously like get it, they subconsciously get it. And see if you're not okay with it, has the seller, I know I'm not supposed to be okay with it, has the buyer. So I don't buy. Next tell. Any hint of apology. Now you can, with the right audience and the right place, you can be cute. You can let them in a little bit but you cannot be apologetic. Again, I see speakers do it all the time. They'll actually apologize. So now I'm only going to take ten minutes of your time and you know, I know you came here to learn and I know, but I need to tell you about, well what have you just told everybody? What I'm doing here now is horrible. It's abusive. It's criminal. I shouldn't be doing it. But buy from me anyway. No, I don't think so. I don't think I'm going to do that. Self-dispiraging humor when shifting into the selling. It has the same effect as the apology. Now if it's in the story before the bridge, it's okay. If it's after the bridge, it's not okay. Because people get that it's a cover mechanism for embarrassment and shame about what you're doing. Disrespectful handling of the physical product. If you are going to handle it. If I'm selling you this, I can't do that when I'm done with it and walk back to the projector. I've seen it. Or any variation of that. I have to handle it lovingly when I display it to you. I have to nicely put it back and make sure it's okay before I move on to the next one. Disrespectful handling of product. Confusion. If you are not going to memorize anything for any presentation. And that may be fine, by the way. Memorize the pitch. Because if you're uncomfortable with it at all, that's the place you're suddenly going to look like in Alzheimer's victim. And it's going to be evident. You sort of have that deer in the headlights look and you're fumbling and, oh, I forgot. I meant to tell you about this. You are destroying the thing. And in many instances, in addition to pitching, you should, like we do in the webcast, cut to a pre-canned, perfect CTA. That now it can't be embarrassed or ashamed or forgetful or confused because you scripted it, you voiced it, you videoed it, so it is perfect. And no reason not to have it playing in a live event, either if you're on a break and they're coming back. Last, hurrying the pitch. So people who have, so he said I was picking on him, I guess I am. The Steve Miller question, right, we have this boundary between content and selling. Here's what those people tend to do. They suck up all the time on the content and they leave themselves almost no time for the pitch and then they race through the pitch. The CTA itself, they race through it. So number one, what does that do? Well, people don't know what the hell they're buying. So the most important part of the presentation is that. That's why you did the rest of it, was to get the opportunity to sell the stuff. So if you're going to short anything, don't short that. Secondly, it telegraphs to people again, you don't feel very good about this. So he's trying to get through this as quick as possible, right, and get the hell out of here. So now they sense that and they don't feel very good about that. And if by the way you have promised the 17 laws of and you somehow are running out of time, just skip some. But don't put yourself at a position to hurry the pitch. Whatever time it's supposed to take, delivered perfectly, you take that time. Now if you're in a multi-speaker environment, you cannot abuse the person behind you. If you do it in our environment and I have an opportunity, I will fix it so you will never work in our world again. So if you are supposed to be done at 10-10 in a multi-speaker environment, you should be done at 10-10. So you've got to take it out of the front. You can't just keep going until you actually get your 22 minutes of CTA. Okay, horribly unprofessional. That's not the answer. So format changes throughout a presentation. So there is, and I'm mostly talking now about video and online, there is direct-to-camera monologue. Person talks to camera. Direct to camera, but with an audience. So you do need to understand, if you watch PBS and you watch the pitch shows on PBS, you will see people make this mistake a lot. So you will see them do what I am doing now. So you will see them kind of walking back and forth and making eye contact with people in the audience and all that. However, you aren't in the audience. You're at home watching it on TV. So we're doing TV, even though there's a live audience. Therefore, your job is to work to the camera. You can do a little bit of this, but you can't do much of it. I didn't get it. I was saved at the Peter Low events by a speaker by the name of Pat Prep, Hurley. So there's 10,000 people in here. First of all, with lights and stuff, I can only see the first two rows. Then it's just a blur. And I'm doing what I would do if I had 200 people in the room. I'm working the room. And I'm trying to see, is he with me? Can I monkey with him? If she want eye contact, that's what I'm doing. And after one of the early ones, I was in San Francisco airport and Pat came over and said, you were great. There's one thing you don't know about what you're doing. That's great. Tell me what I don't know. She says, everybody but the first two rows, not only can you see them, they can't see you. They're watching the jumbo trunks. You're on television. You're not on stage. Therefore, the camera. So you don't take the product and do this. You do this. You're on camera. You're doing TV and unfortunately you're doing a one-camera shoot. I said, I got it. And I watch people flounder and fail in that environment often because they didn't know what I now knew because she told me. You can usually improve some of these results the minute you fix that. I didn't volunteer it to everybody. I only volunteered it when I was getting paid by somebody. I'm not as kind as Pat. But you automatically improve their results when you fix that. Because most of the people were watching them on a big TV. So monologue, direct to camera, with or without an audience. One-to-one interview. You can do. You, you're the pitch person, whoever that is, and a host. One-to-one interview. Be very careful you don't see this is no good because you sink. No good. The seats right now on the... Who show is it? It's Kimmel. The seats on Kimmel's show are horrendous. I mean, people sink into them and little guys like Tom Cruise just... There's a head. I mean, you know, it's like, it's like you see in Florida, you know, cars go past you. There's nothing there but a head and two little hands on a steering wheel. You know, there's nobody else there. They're small and they're low. So those are no good, right? So you got to make sure if you do interviews, and usually most people, they're energy level dips when they are comfortably seated. So the best interviews are like on these, if you're going to do them, even at a live event. If you have somebody who's, if you decide to do this, if you have somebody at one of your events who has something great to sell, it's of great value, but they can't sell with a shit, you know, and maybe they can't even speak with a shit. Then you can do an interview on stage and you can carry that. Now, if you do too much of it, then you negate your ability to sell anything, but so the one-to-panel interview, okay, you can do that in an environment, obviously. You can do the PowerPoints presentation. I will caution you about this in a live environment. One of the quickest ways to suck energy out of the room is to turn on the PowerPoints presentation. Everybody's experience with PowerPoint speakers, most of them are horrible. The better, the quicker way to drain the energy out of the room is go to PowerPoints presentation and dim the lights. That'll do it. That'll do it. They say, here comes the college professor. Uh-oh. Now, I'm not good enough to work without help, but you will, I'll die before you'll see me doing PowerPoints. It just ain't gonna happen. And I will tell you, did Mark use PowerPoints? Mark used PowerPoints, right, which did not help him stay on track at all, by the way. So, it's primary purpose, didn't even deliver itself, right? So, he and I have had this discussion for years. The day he switched to PowerPoints, his selling effectiveness on stage dropped by half. And it's never come back. If I could wean him off PowerPoints, he'd sell twice as much stuff. Physical demonstration in the studio, physical demonstration in the field, so now you have to show it on video, okay? And you can cut the video on a monitor. Just don't go there too long. You see, we do it in a lot of infomercials. Now you can do it with Skype, so you can cut the Skype. Just don't do it too long, okay? Because they will decide they don't want to pay attention. Length and breakdown of presentations. As I said, an infomercial is 28 and a half minutes. The DVD sent to the Homer office can be considerably longer, as long as you don't bore them and all of that. I will mostly do 45 to 90 minute presentations if I'm going to get somebody to request a DVD and send them a DVD. Online, the infomercial length all the way up to an hour is about right. The people who tell you seven minutes, they're full of crap. It has an example when you break it down, so an infomercial 28 and a half, a fairly typical format is you've got three segments. You really only have 21 and a half minutes left after you take out the CTAs. The first CTA is the longest, second CTA is shorter than the first. The last CTA is the shortest of everything. So you've got to break down your timing, your time schedule of any of these presentations and then figure out how you apportion it. You don't... See, this is not like a sales letter. So when you write copy for a sales letter, my approach is I write until I'm done. I'm being... Whatever it takes. It's 28 pages, fine. If it's 36 pages, fine. If it's 52 pages, fine. It's right until I take... Well, here we have finite time. So you've got to create a time frame of your acts, your sections, your CTAs, and then you've got to fit things into them. Webcasts, you can obviously do three or four hours. We're doing them very successfully. Some people are doing longer. Again, you've got to break out your CTAs. Now you have X amount of time left. What are you going to do? Are you going to do three segments? Or you've got to do four segments? You've got to create a schematic that you're going to fill in the boxes. Preview seminars. My preference is three hours. My own preference and experience is much more than three. You shouldn't need... Now, I know guys are doing all day, but I think it's excessive. And we used for success track and then later for Jay Geyer. I'm trying to take fill down to three. So in three hours, you have the ability to have a first break where nobody sells anything, a second break where all the sales is done, and they come back and you still have a shot at them at the end. If you're speaking to sell, my own. I'm only telling you me. But for me, I can't get it done in less than 90 minutes. Now I wound up at 75 on the success events and we paid a price for it. I can't do it in less than 90 and do it effectively. For a product like magnetic marketing, again, if you go back through what we talked about today, there's certain things you have to know. There's certain things you have to have agreed to in sequence before you will buy that product. If I have to cheat any of that, you really can't buy the product because we haven't had the sequential agreements. I prefer two hours without a break, three to three and a half. I'll stick a break in it because it allows me to create more than one selling break or a non-selling break and then a break. So if you are speaking for other people, by the way, you got to fight for what works for you. So I got an engagement. We only want to give you 90 minutes. I said, no, I'm with Banks. I got to him two hours. I want two hours. Screw somebody else. I don't care about them. I care about me. I want two hours, so I got two hours. If you're doing video, please think about some of these things. One, if you're doing it in studio, really think through whether you want to do green screen or whether you want to build sets. Nothing you do on green screen looks as good as a real set. And we'll get to it, but this is TV. So very little is done on TV. On green screen, it's even today. They have sets. You can actually go to Hollywood and see them. There's a richness to it. There's a depth to it. There's objects in it, dressing a set in a way that symbolizes things and is meaningful to people and adds some interest to what's going on is important. Locations. Lots of choices for locations besides the studio. You might shoot in your house. You might shoot in your office. You can go to a factory, to a place, to a hotel, to an iconic site. So if you're doing a health pitch, you can go stand in front of the Mayo Clinic until they catch you. You can go, I mean, you can pick some iconic, if you're doing a get rich thing, you can go stand in front of the Statue of Liberty and you can talk about financial liberty. I mean, there's iconic sites that at least for a short time, you can go film there quick and get out before anybody asks you about permits or any of that other stuff. Plains, trains, boats, automobiles. You know, there's Seinfeld's show now of comedians drinking coffee in cars or whatever and they sit in the car and talk to each other and drink coffee and people are tuning in and watching it on a webcast. Outdoors. Now, outdoor shoots add a lot of interest and they can allow you to make a point and to symbolize things. I will tell you, they are a giant pain in the butt. You got to allow some time and you got to have people who know what they're doing. They are pretty painful most of the time. I shot an infomercial, you've never seen it and you won't. But I shot an infomercial with Gary Halberd. So many of you know Gary. In Key West, on site. What a horror show. Of course, it's Key West, so you know, no member of the public is going to be cooperative with this at all. They are going to engage, you know. And a lot of people, not the tourists, but the people who live in Key West are pretty much there because they're not allowed to live anywhere else. This is like the last stop for them other than a prison. That's who's there. And a number of them are pretty high and a number of them are pretty drunk all day long. It's hot, it's humid. We shot one scene, Halberd and the host, it was Don Drysdale. Don's dead now, but baseball player. In big high canvas chairs, on the marina with the yachts, you know, behind and seagulls and shit. And it's only, this is like five minutes. I need two questions, two answers. That's all I need. It took me all day. Just about the time you get it, a seagull poops right on his head. And Gary goes, hey, he pooped on my head. I'm going, fuck. I mean, it is right. I'm down to like three words. The yacht, right behind Drysdale, somebody hits the button and disgorges the sewage. Stuff's coming out. Gotta start over. We're shooting. How many of you, Gary's significant other for many years, your name is Paulette. Anybody know Paulette, anybody? So Paulette loved, just a great woman, but not smart. Sort of intuitively smart, you know, but not just not smart. So this scene is, she drives up in front of the hotel and Gary's Rolls Royce gets out, walks to the hotel. This is not a speaking part. This is B-roll, drive up, stop car, get out, walk to hotel. It's all we need, right? All day. All day. She pulls up in the wrong direction and gets out on the non-hotel side. And now, where do I go? We're filming, you know. She pulls up, gets out, stands there. Paulette, you know. Now we got a cue card. Says, walk to hotel. This is what's going on. I mean, it took all day. Drysdale, my dad, was a pretty good drinker. So he was only good until about noon because the heat and the humidity and the booze in his body from the night before, I was a pretty good boozer myself. And I mean, you get a bunch of scotch in your body and go to sleep with it. You were going to sweat it out the next day and you are less and less able to not look like you're drenched in sweat as the day goes on. I mean, so outdoor stuff is just ugly. But it adds an enormous amount of an interest to a video. And when you go to people's natural habitats and shoot there, so you just got to decide how much pain you know you're willing to endure. The live seminar stuff with audiences works. Just make sure you got multiple cameras so you got stuff to cover and you shoot. Everybody laughing and everybody clapping. And if you're going to shoot over a period of time, you got to get them back in the seats they were in after they go to the bathroom. Okay, because if they switch seats, now you got problems. You can't let anybody leave early. They got to be committed to be there because then you got empty seats. So you got to think through the audience thing. Technical procedural stuff, right? An activity where they're going to watch you do something. You got to allow enough time to shoot that 56 different angles and ways and all of that filming. There's one camera shoots, which is what most people do for web stuff. Two camera shoots, three camera shoots, four camera shoots. They're stationary and moving. If you move while you get recorded, that sometimes you can't do it. People, they can't walk and talk at the same time. It's problematic for them. Other people can do it really, really well. If you're filming an old person, sometimes that's not good because they sort of shuffle and stuff. A boom mic, if you can afford it, is a great thing to have at a studio shoot because you get an extra moving camera angle when you go into post production. Extra shots to use in post. Biggest thing I'll tell you, if you are shooting something that you are not just going to live broadcast or you are not just going to slap up on the web as a one and done. But you actually are shooting something that you want to live with for a while. All your great work is going to be done in post. Post production is where everything happens. And you can't have enough footage. So just about the time you think you got it right, shoot some more. Get the boats with nobody sitting in front of them. Get the boat moving in. Get the boat moving out. Get cover shot, cover shot, cover shot, cover shot. Because he may not ever get the two sentences together the way we need him. But he'll have all the pieces. And if you don't have anything to cover him with while you continue to hear the sound, you are screwed. You're stuck with what's called a sharp cut. You can see it. I cut it and just slapped him together and took six words out. It's visible. There needs to be cover. The boat. You've got to be able to come back. The other person. That's why three camera shoots are better. You've got one stationary wide shot. I've got a close-up on Ben and I've got a close-up on him for the whole time. So I wind up with the ability to cover a couple of different ways. Product beauty shots. Shoot the stuff you're selling. Active. Somebody handling it with nice hands. Moving it around. Stationary. Get a bunch of product beauty shots. Get a lot of B-roll. Get the product used if there's a way to use it. If it's a notebook with pages in it, get shots of somebody opening the notebook, looking through pages. If they're going to listen to CDs, get some video of them taking the CD out of the package, putting it in the CD player. You won't know you want it necessarily until you get the post. But you can't go back and get most of this. If there's a celebrity, if you rented a celebrity, make sure you get every possible thing you would ever want from that celebrity now. Because you can't get them back. So if you want them to handle the product, you've got to think through all of that. If using a seminar or a studio audience. You can do, as I said, volunteers, you can rent to order. You do want to try and match the audience, if they're going to be visible, to your audience who's going to be buying. And there's no point in having people in the visible audience who don't match up with your buyers. So if you're selling weight loss to women, there's no reason to have a guy in the audience. Stupid. I mean, you've got to know who your market is. If you are candidly selling and 90% of your customers and 90% of the people you are driving to the webcast are white, no reason to have, you're not going to get the diversity award from your webcast. No reason to have a black guy in an Asian and American Indian with a feather. There's no reason to have any of them. Vice versa, if a significant number of customers of yours are black, you can't have all white folks in the audience. They've got to see themselves. If all your buyers are 55 and over, and you've got 20-year-olds in there because, you know, your kid and three of his buddies were available to be in the audience, bad idea. So you want to match to your buyer. Get more than you need. Some of them won't show up. Some of them, which is really bad, and you've got to wedge them in the back, but some of them will leave early even though they agreed not to leave early. Some of them will get sick. Somebody will be in your audience and they'll be scratching themselves the whole time. You're going to have to get them out of there and replace them. Clear and complete instructions before they come. And a babysitter. Do not have an audience without at least one person assigned to manage that audience. They are not buicks that you park in position. Put the parking gear on and let them idle. That's not how human beings are. Have extra wardrobe on hand for them. A, despite your instructions, they may show up. He is going to show up in a purple polka dot sweatshirt that says F-U on it. And when you turn it inside out, it says F-U inside it. You need to be able to dress that guy. The first thing he's going to do when he gets there is he's got a white shirt on and he's going to spill his coffee all over it. So you've got to have extra wardrobe. Make him happy. Audiences need to be happy. So you've got to make him happy. The babysitter has to be able to interact with them. You need to come out and engage them. The presenter has to play well with the people. The worst presenter in the world to put with a live audience is somebody that doesn't play well with the people. So we're shooting a show. I won't name them. Famous speaker you would know. We're shooting a live audience show and we have stocked it all with his real testimonials. At great difficulty. So it's like 250 people in this audience. It looks like the whole environment kind of looks like a TV, like an Oprah show or something. And he's on stage doing his thing. And act two is interaction with the audience. And the host who's a celebrity is like Phil Donahue used to be. So he's out in the audience with the microphone. This is what's going on. And this woman stands up and starts telling her story. She's like excited and crying. And she burps. This is not choreographed. You couldn't choreograph it. You couldn't pay enough. She pushes herself past the celebrity. She runs to the stage to give our guy a big hug. And he does this. You'll never get it again. And the truth is he don't like anybody. And he don't like his customers. And he don't like physical contact. Better not to make him interact with a live audience. If you are dealing with inexperienced presenters, be very careful about doing cue cards or prompters. They lock on them. You can tell they're locked on them. Boom! You can tell they like follow the bobbing ball. It's very hard to disengage them and fix that. You are far better off if you got an experienced host, put him on prompter, but just overshoot. Just shoot a ton with them and clean it up in post. Be very careful about asking amateurs to use pro tools. You're better off letting them sit at the tall table on the stools with notes and everybody sees they're dealing with notes. Then you are having them tuned in to the prompter. Don't make demands they're not going to be able to do. Know what you got to deal with if you're shooting testimonials, if you're shooting another presenter. Limit the rehearsal if you're dealing with inexperienced presenters. They tend to get worse, not better. One run through and then take what you can get. If you're using professional presenters, then it's more about real management of them. And if they're hired, if they're showing up to shoot, you need time to befriend them. So they like you. Little known secret about celebrities and I've treated very well. And so they show up braced for a really bad experience with a jackass. That's their attitude coming in the door. And often their behavior will reflect it unless you have fixed that. If live to broadcast webcast and you're really serious about what you're doing and or there are regulatory issues with what you're doing, there's a lot you may want to, you may want tape delay just like in live television. You do want live switch, which means some of the editing is done as you go. People who are good at it can do a lot of it. If you're booking a studio, talk to them about their live switch capabilities. I said you want to do post and plan to get more than one use out of whatever you do. What if the first use doesn't work? So when we took money from people producing infomercial, I always strategize, let's make sure we get web video that you'll be able to use on the web. Can we get a DVD out of this? Can we get five little videos you'll be able to use? What's all the different stuff we can get out of this day of production, two days of production, whatever? So if the main thing you did turns out to be a clinker, you have other opportunities to do things with it. We are out of clock. There are a couple of things we have not covered, but there are a lot of notes about them. So I think you will be fine. And there is a bonus section of additional samples for you and your manual that I do not even have copies of. And now I have lost my... here it is. So we are done. After a half an hour break, huh? They are comfortable in theater seats. No good. If you can sell in that environment, you can sell on the top of a bus as it is driving across country, leaning over and yelling in the window.
